summary: Requires SMB signing for outbound client connections by setting both RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature under LanmanWorkstation. Microsoft directly documents the same registry key, value names, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for this SMB client signing posture.

setting.name: SMB Client Signing Requirement
setting.casual_explanation: SMB signing helps Windows verify that network file-sharing traffic was not altered in transit. Requiring it makes the SMB client stricter when talking to servers.
setting.why_people_change_it: People require SMB signing to harden file sharing against tampering and downgrade-style issues. They relax it only for compatibility with older or weaker SMB servers.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Requiring SMB signing is safer, but some old devices or servers may fail to connect, and there can be a performance cost on slower systems or links.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents both RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanWorkstation\Parameters. The current app sets both to 1, which matches the stricter client-signing baseline.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows determines the SMB client signing requirement based on its built-in release baseline.

allowed_values.label: Signing not required
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB client does not require signing for outbound connections.

allowed_values.label: Signing required
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB client requires signing for outbound connections.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows determines whether SMB client signing is enabled by default for compatible connections.

allowed_values.label: Signing not enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB client does not enable signing for normal negotiated connections.

allowed_values.label: Signing enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB client enables signing for compatible connections.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes RequireSecuritySignature = 1 and EnableSecuritySignature = 1, matching the stricter SMB client signing posture described by Microsoft.

windows_defaults.label: Version-dependent Windows SMB signing baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows SMB client releases with signing support

states.rationale: Microsoft changes SMB signing defaults across Windows releases and scenarios.
Microsoft changes SMB signing defaults across Windows releases and scenarios.

windows_defaults.notes: This record avoids flattening Windows defaults into one static numeric baseline because Microsoft has changed client-signing defaults over time.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: Users who want the release baseline chosen by Windows
People who are not ready to force an SMB client override
avoid_for: Security baselines that explicitly require SMB client signing

states.rationale: Leave the explicit client signing override absent.
Leave the explicit client signing override absent.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its own client-signing baseline for the installed release.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a documented hard requirement for client-side SMB signing.
tradeoffs: Behavior can differ across Windows versions and network scenarios.

recommended_profiles.label: Require signing
intended_for: People who want explicit SMB integrity protection

states.rationale: Require signing for outbound SMB client connections.
Keep SMB signing enabled for compatible connections alongside the requirement bit.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the app to force the stricter SMB client signing posture.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You still talk to old SMB endpoints that cannot handle signing correctly.
tradeoffs: Some legacy SMB peers may stop working or perform worse.

evidence.title: Microsoft SMB signing overview
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the LanmanWorkstation RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature registry values and explains SMB signing defaults and behavior.

evidence.title: Microsoft network client: Digitally sign communications (always)
evidence.summary: Official Microsoft security option guidance for the client-side always-sign SMB policy.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes RequireSecuritySignature = 1 and EnableSecuritySignature = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManWorkstation\Parameters -> RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature, both with Data 0 (disable) and 1 (enable). The same page also explains that SMB2+ signing is ultimately controlled by RequireSecuritySignature, while EnableSecuritySignature is ignored for SMB2+ but still a documented runtime setting on the same surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Registry key: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManWorkstation\Parameters; Registry value: RequireSecuritySignature; Data Type: REG_DWORD; Data: 0 (disable), 1 (enable). Registry key: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManWorkstation\Parameters; Registry value: EnableSecuritySignature; Data Type: REG_DWORD; Data: 0 (disable), 1 (enable).
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft documents both client-side SMB signing registry values on the same LanManWorkstation runtime path the app writes. The article also notes that EnableSecuritySignature is ignored for SMB2+ and that SMB2.02 and later signing is controlled by being required or not.
